macro_segments.title: The Debate on Admission (Hoda'ah) and Exemption from Kenas: Rav vs. Shmuel and the Requirement of Obligating Oneself in Principal

micro_segments.title: Rav's position: admission exempts from kenas even if witnesses come later
Shmuel's position: admission doesn't obligate but witnesses do
Nuanced view: admission exempts only if it obligates oneself in principal (keren)

macro_segments.title: Support from Brayta: The Case of Admitting to Theft While Denying Slaughter and Sale

micro_segments.title: The scenario: witnesses approaching, defendant admits theft but denies slaughter and sale
Why the Brayta gives the example of admitting to theft: proving admission to principal exempts from kefel
Gemara's objection: perhaps the Brayta is only teaching a novelty about that case, not proving our position
The novelty in the case: one admits to theft but denies slaughter, yet remains exempt from kefel even if witnesses come to the slaughter
Explanation: Torah says pay four and five; removing one payment removes both, so admission to principal eliminates kefel obligation

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Alternative Explanation: The Brayta May Not Prove the Hoda'ah Position

micro_segments.title: Gemara suggests the debate may be a tannaitic dispute about admission that doesn't obligate in principal
Case of witnesses to theft disproven (zomemim): defendant must pay kenas only; owner recovers nothing
Case of witnesses to slaughter disproven: defendant pays owners kefel, witnesses pay defendant three
Sumchus's position: witnesses pay two, defendant pays three—reconstructing the logical scenario

macro_segments.title: Complex Scenario: Admission to Theft Before Zomemim Witnesses vs. Sincere Admission to Slaughter

micro_segments.title: Setup: two witnesses claim theft, defendant admits theft and slaughter; defendant then proves witnesses saw him elsewhere (zomemim)
Rabbanan's view: admission to slaughter still counts despite the false witnesses, exempting from kenas
Sumchus's view: admission to slaughter made only in reaction to false witnesses, so admission to slaughter is not valid
Rav Acha bar Rav Ika's reinterpretation: the real debate is about testimony that cannot be proven zomemim
Owner then brings Reuven and Shimon as witnesses to theft and slaughter

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Testimony That Cannot Be Disproven (Edut She'ei Atah Yachol Lehazimah)

micro_segments.title: Rabbanan hold undisproven testimony is disqualified; admission supporting it makes testimony invalid
Sumchus holds undisproven testimony is still valid; defendant pays full obligation to owner
Gemara concludes everyone agrees undisproven testimony is invalid, so Sumchus's position is rejected

macro_segments.title: Moving to the Mishnah: Theft, Sanctification, and Slaughter—The Question of Whether Sanctification Constitutes a Sale

micro_segments.title: Mishnah case: steals, sanctifies as korban, then slaughters—does he pay four and five?
Gemara's question: Why isn't sanctification like a sale (mechira)?
Answer via Rabbi Shimon: kodashim that create liability (chayav be'achrayutan) are still considered under one's control, not fully transferred
Distinction between kodashim kalim (light sacrifices) and kodashei kodashim (holy of holies) in terms of when sanctification exempts

macro_segments.title: Why Sanctification Is Not a Mechira: Status Change vs. Ownership Transfer

micro_segments.title: Explanation: when sold to ordinary person, ownership fully transfers; when sanctified, still called 'one's korban,' remains associated with owner
Deeper point: sanctification confers kedusha (status) rather than transferring ownership; korban status is about purpose, not possession
Theological implication: kodashei bedek ha'bayit (donations) and kodashei karbanot (sacrifices) involve different types of sanctity
Tosafot: shinui hashem (change of name) and shinui reshut (change of domain) do occur, but korban remains partially under owner's auspices

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Addendum: Stealing a Korban from One Who Is Liable for Its Replacement

micro_segments.title: Apparent contradiction in Rabbi Shimon's statement: if chayav be'achrayutan (one liable for replacement), you must pay four and five
Clarification: Rabbi Shimon speaks of stealing a korban from owner's house, not of stealing and then sanctifying oneself
Kodashim chayav be'achrayutan are considered the owner's property because he must replace them if lost; therefore theft pays four and five
Kodashim not chayav be'achrayutan are not considered owner's property; theft is exempt as if stealing from hekdesh

macro_segments.title: The Problem of Slaughtering a Korban Outside the Beis HaMikdash: Can It Still Qualify as Shechicha Re'uya?

micro_segments.title: Problem: Rabbi Shimon says shechicha that is not fitting (not re'uya) does not incur four and five; slaughtering korban outside Beis HaMikdash is assur be'hana'ah
First answer (Rav Dimi): defendant brought korban to Beis HaMikdash and slaughtered it for owner's name, making it valid shechicha; blood then spilled, showing it wasn't credited to owner
Second answer (Ravin): defendant slaughtered inside Beis HaMikdash but not for owner's name; meat could be eaten, so shechicha is re'uya, and he pays because no keren (return) occurred
Third answer (Reish Lakish): animal had a mum (blemish) and was slaughtered outside; not violating shechicha outside law, but still shechicha of sanctified item (opening to further discussion)
